risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were assigned to the two treatment groups according to a randomization list generated by the Biostatistics Department of MEDA Pharma. The random allocation was balanced using an undisclosed block size of six." Comment: The paper states that a randomization list was used and that allocation was balanced with blocks of six, but it does not describe how the sequence itself was generated (e.g., computer random number generator, random number table, etc.). Per the domain criteria, blocked randomization without the generation method is insufficient to judge truly random sequence generation.

Quote: "Patients were assigned to the two treatment groups according to a randomization list generated by the Biostatistics Department of MEDA Pharma. The random allocation was balanced using an undisclosed block size of six. The investigators and the monitor received sealed envelopes to enable decoding the individual blinded treatment in case of emergency." Comment: The paper does not explicitly describe central allocation or SNOSE, but it states that treatment assignment was based on a randomization list generated separately by the Biostatistics Department, and the investigators/monitor only received sealed envelopes for emergency unblinding. This implies the recruiters did not have access to the allocation sequence before enrollment, so concealment is judged low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled" and "film-coated tablets containing 600 mg ALA ... or matching placebo tablets with increased amounts of cellulose and lactose that were identical in appearance". Comment: Participants and treating personnel were explicitly blinded, and the active and placebo tablets were matched in appearance. Although some outcomes were clinical neuropathy scores, the trial design indicates successful blinding and there is no indication that allocation knowledge would have been available to influence care or outcome assessment.

Quote: "Experienced, trained, and certified ... physicians evaluated the NIS and NSC. Study physicians had participated in training sessions and actual examination of patients under observation using a formal certification process. The nerve conduction, quantitative sensory tests (QSTs), and autonomic tests were performed by trained and certified personnel ... All results were interactively evaluated by the Reading and Quality Assurance Centers (at Mayo Clinic and Health Partners)." Comment: The outcome assessors were clinicians and trained certified personnel, with central quality assurance; the study was double-blind and the main outcomes included clinical scores plus nerve conduction/QST measures. Because the likely assessors were blinded under the trial's double-blind design and many key measurements were objective or standardized, knowledge of allocation was unlikely to have influenced outcome measurement.

Quote: "More than 40% of the participants from each group discontinued treatment/placebo. Results for all outcomes at 2 years from randomisation were reported for 421 participants (214 in the ALA group and 207 in the placebo group)." Comment: Attrition was very large (>20%) in both arms over the long trial, and although the abstracted note says discontinuation was similar across groups, the paper/record does not provide enough detail here on reasons for missingness or whether outcome data were handled in a way that would avoid bias. Large dropout over 4 years could plausibly affect the outcome estimates, so this is high risk.
